preamble: Fortition and devoicing of voiced uvular fricative [ʁ] to voiceless uvular stop [q]. The shift involves two simultaneous changes: fricative → stop (fortition) and voiced → voiceless (devoicing). In Berber languages this is triggered by gemination: singleton /ʁ/ strengthens to /qː/ in its geminate form, a pattern documented in the Ayt Ndhir variety of Central Atlas Tamazight. A parallel conditioned pathway occurs in Tashlhiyt (Tachelhit) Berber, where voiced fricatives partially or fully devoice before voiceless obstruents.

examples.note: In the Ayt Ndhir variety of Central Atlas Tamazight, the geminate correspondent of singleton /ʁ/ is /qː/ rather than /ʁː/. This is part of a broader pattern in which several voiced fricatives and approximants map to voiceless obstruents in geminate position (e.g., /ʒ/ → /d͡ʒː/, /w/ → /ɣʷː/). Some native Berber words preserve /ʁː/ instead, showing this is not yet categorical.

In Tashlhiyt Berber, voiced fricatives including [ʁ] are fully or partially devoiced when immediately followed by a voiceless obstruent. In such clusters, [ʁ] approaches [χ] or, combined with fortition in geminate contexts, may surface as [q].

sources: Ridouane, R. (2014). Tashlhiyt Berber. Journal of the International Phonetic Association, 44(2), 207–221.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
